channel.title: Daily Digest
channel.description: Curated tech blog summaries, inspired by Andrej Karpathy's reading list

item.title: Help I'm being persecuted
item.description: This article explores the psychological and social dynamics of individuals experiencing a perceived sense of persecution. It examines the human tendency to fixate on external crises or conflicts, often referred to as 'rubbernecking,' and analyzes why people feel drawn to these narratives.

item.title: Weekly Update 495
item.description: Troy Hunt reflects on the evolution of Have I Been Pwned's architecture over time. The system has shifted from a simple database setup to a complex infrastructure utilizing serverless functions, edge computing, and advanced storage solutions.

item.title: Your Startup Is Probably Dead On Arrival
item.description: Steve Blank warns that startups founded over two years ago face high failure risks due to rapidly changing market assumptions. He advises founders to pause operational tasks like coding or fundraising to critically reassess their business environment and strategy.

item.title: Hedley Davis has died
item.description: This post serves as an obituary for Hedley Davis, a former Commodore engineer known for his work on the Amiga line and the prototype SX-500. It briefly touches on his contributions to early portable computing and his later career, including work on the Xbox 360.

item.title: You Might Debate It — If You Could See It
item.description: The article discusses how design and coding guidelines are increasingly being 'baked' into LLMs as opaque system instructions. It argues that while teams might debate such guidelines if presented explicitly, they are often silently adopted through AI-generated outputs, highlighting a risk of losing control over creative standards.

item.title: Esqueleto Tutorial
item.description: This post provides a tutorial for Esqueleto, a type-safe library for writing SQL queries in Haskell. It guides users through setup, basic usage, and common query patterns.

item.title: Powers don’t clear fractions
item.description: This article discusses a mathematical property stating that if a real number has a finite but nonzero fractional part, all of its positive integer powers will also have a fractional part. It references a concise proof demonstrating that such powers can never result in an integer.

item.title: Tone row operations
item.description: This post explains the mathematical group of operations applied to twelve-tone rows in music theory. It defines the four primary transformations: prime (P), retrograde (R), inversion (I), and retrograde-inversion (RI).

item.title: Windows stack limit checking retrospective: x86-32 also known as i386, second try
item.description: This article is a technical retrospective examining how Windows handles stack limit checking on x86-32 (i386) architectures. It specifically discusses the challenges of managing the return address predictor in the context of stack depth constraints.

item.title: My homelab will be down for at least 20 days
item.description: The author announces a prolonged 20-day downtime for their homelab. They humorously express their frustration regarding the situation.

item.title: Pluralistic: William Gibson vs Margaret Thatcher (17 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow analyzes William Gibson's famous aphorism, 'the street finds its own uses for things,' as a statement of technopolitics. He argues that users can reclaim and repurpose corporate technology for virtuous ends, challenging the idea that tools inherently dictate their own oppressive outcomes.

item.title: Quoting Ken Jin
item.description: Ken Jin reports that the CPython JIT compiler has reached its performance milestones ahead of schedule for macOS and Linux. The upcoming Python 3.15 alpha version shows significant speed improvements over traditional interpreters.

item.title: GPT-5.4 mini and GPT-5.4 nano, which can describe 76,000 photos for $52
item.description: OpenAI has introduced the GPT-5.4 mini and nano models, which offer improved performance and speed compared to previous iterations. The article highlights their competitive pricing, with the nano model positioning itself as a highly cost-effective option for large-scale tasks like image description.

item.title: Quoting Tim Schilling
item.description: Tim Schilling argues that over-reliance on LLMs for open-source contributions to Django harms the community by removing human understanding and authentic communication. He emphasizes that AI should serve as a complementary tool rather than a replacement for personal effort in technical projects.

item.title: Subagents
item.description: The article discusses the 'subagent' pattern in AI engineering, which helps overcome LLM context window limitations by delegating specific tasks to fresh agent instances. It uses Claude Code as a primary example to illustrate how subagents explore code repositories to manage complex workflows efficiently.

item.title: Introducing Mistral Small 4
item.description: Mistral AI has released Mistral Small 4, an Apache 2 licensed, 119B parameter Mixture-of-Experts model that integrates reasoning, multimodal, and agentic coding capabilities. The author explores the model's performance via the API and highlights the simultaneous release of Leanstral, a specialized model for the Lean 4 formal verification language.

item.title: Use subagents and custom agents in Codex
item.description: OpenAI has officially launched subagents for its Codex platform, allowing users to leverage specialized agents like 'explorer' and 'worker' for parallel tasks. The update also enables the creation of custom agents via TOML files, providing flexibility in model selection and instruction sets for complex workflows.

item.title: Why I Love FreeBSD
item.description: This article reflects on the author's twenty-year journey with FreeBSD, highlighting how its core philosophy, stability, and community have profoundly influenced their approach to system design. It serves as a personal testimonial to the enduring value and reliability of the platform.

item.title: Updates OpenTK: alerts voor antwoorden op kamervragen, betere kamerstukdossiers en meer!
item.description: This post announces updates to the OpenTK platform, which improves parliamentary documentation navigation by making document and case numbers clickable. These enhancements follow user feedback regarding improved cross-referencing capabilities within the system.

item.title: Food, Software, and Trade-offs
item.description: The author uses food analogies to illustrate that quality is subjective and dependent on context. He argues that all decisions, whether in cooking or software development, involve inherent trade-offs that cannot be ignored or generalized.

item.title: Polynomial Time Factoring Algorithm
item.description: The author argues that AI will soon discover a polynomial-time algorithm for integer factoring, potentially rendering current asymmetric cryptography obsolete. They further speculate that quantum speedups are essentially classical, suggesting that P equals BQP and that traditional encryption will soon be easily broken.

item.title: The optimized self and the life that got away
item.description: The article explores the psychological tension between the pursuit of an 'optimized self' and the reality of the paths we leave behind. It reflects on the trade-offs inherent in modern self-improvement and the acceptance of the life that remains unlived.

item.title: Twelve-tone composition
item.description: This article discusses twelve-tone composition as a technique to avoid tonal patterns in atonal music. It explains how composers use a specific permutation of the 12 chromatic notes to impose discipline and structure on their work.

item.title: Windows stack limit checking retrospective: PowerPC
item.description: This post examines the historical implementation of stack limit checking on the PowerPC architecture within Windows. It provides a technical retrospective on the specific low-level mechanics used to manage stack overflow protection.

item.title: Some updates to ActivityBot
item.description: Terence Eden provides updates on ActivityBot, a lightweight, single-file PHP tool designed for creating Mastodon bots. The project supports the ActivityPub protocol and demonstrates its functionality through active examples like automated post bots.

item.title: Pluralistic: Tools vs uses (16 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow explains how legislative loopholes are often deliberate tools for corruption rather than accidental drafting errors. He cites specific examples, such as the 2017 Trump tax bill, to illustrate how lawmakers insert amendments that provide massive financial windfalls for their wealthy campaign donors.

item.title: The Loyalty Oath Crusade
item.description: The article uses a scene from Catch-22 to illustrate the absurdity of bureaucratic overreach. It highlights how mindless conformity to complex, illogical rules can paralyze an organization until an authoritative voice dares to challenge the status quo.

item.title: Shower Thought: Git Teleportation
item.description: The article uses a humorous analogy by applying Git version control workflows to the concept of sci-fi teleportation. It frames planetary missions as feature branches that must be merged back to the master branch after cleanup, illustrating complex concepts through familiar technical commands.

item.title: Quoting A member of Anthropic’s alignment-science team
item.description: This post highlights an Anthropic researcher's perspective on using 'blackmail exercises' to demonstrate AI misalignment risks to policymakers. These practical simulations are designed to make abstract safety concerns more tangible and urgent for decision-makers.

item.title: Quoting Guilherme Rambo
item.description: Guilherme Rambo explains that the MacBook Neo's software-based camera indicator light is managed within the secure enclave. This ensures that even a kernel-level exploit cannot bypass the indicator, providing security comparable to a hardware light.

item.title: Coding agents for data analysis
item.description: This blog post provides a comprehensive workshop handout on using AI coding agents for data analysis, specifically tailored for data journalists. It covers practical applications ranging from database querying and data cleaning to web scraping and visualization using Python and SQLite.

item.title: How coding agents work
item.description: This article explains the fundamental mechanics of coding agents, which function as software harnesses for Large Language Models. It describes how LLMs process information using tokens and highlights the roles of prompts and completions in agentic workflows.

item.title: What is agentic engineering?
item.description: Simon Willison defines 'agentic engineering' as the practice of building software using coding agents capable of writing and executing code in a loop. He explains that the ability for an agent to run tools to achieve a goal, rather than just generating text, is the core capability that enables this new software development paradigm.

item.title: Why Claude's new 1M context length is a big deal
item.description: Anthropic has introduced a 1 million token context window for its Claude Opus and Sonnet models, marking a significant technological advancement. Notably, this substantial increase in capacity comes without any additional cost to the users.

item.title: Langford series
item.description: This post introduces the Langford pairing problem, a mathematical challenge where numbers 1 through n appear twice in a sequence with specific spacing requirements. It explores the unique properties and constraints of these sequences using the example of n=12.

item.title: Book Review: Robots in Space - The Secret Lives of Our Planetary Explorers by Dr Ezzy Pearson ★★★⯪☆
item.description: This book review explores Dr. Ezzy Pearson's work, which chronicles the history of robotic planetary exploration. The text highlights the technical challenges of landing robots on other worlds, the individuals behind these missions, and the complex political landscape influencing space exploration.

item.title: BertVote Gemeenteraadsverkiezingen 2026
item.description: Bert Hubert discusses the upcoming 2026 Dutch municipal elections and his transition from the 'NerdVote' campaign. Because he is personally running as a candidate, he provides a curated list of recommended candidates he knows personally instead of a formal campaign.

item.title: Guided Meditation for Developers
item.description: This piece uses the format of a guided meditation to address common developer stressors like npm dependency bloat, deprecated packages, and unresolved GitHub issues. It encourages software engineers to practice mindfulness and acceptance regarding the uncontrollable aspects of their codebase and work environment.

item.title: How to use the Qwen 3.5 LLMs to OCR documents
item.description: This guide demonstrates how to utilize Qwen 3.5 open-weight models for optical character recognition (OCR) on scanned PDFs. It covers implementation options ranging from local deployment on consumer hardware to cost-effective API usage via OpenRouter.

item.title: Last-Run Syndication
item.source: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.
item.description: This article explores the decline of the first-run syndication television model, which once allowed independent stations to thrive and kept popular shows relevant. It explains the historical economic incentives that made syndication profitable for producers and advertisers, and examines why major companies like NBCUniversal are now abandoning the practice.

item.title: Reading List 03/14/26
item.description: This digest covers a diverse range of current events, including geopolitical risks in the Strait of Hormuz and housing policy shifts in the US. It also highlights corporate developments from Honda and Travis Kalanick, alongside critical infrastructure issues in Corpus Christi.

item.title: What’s Going On with FAIR Package Manager
item.description: The FAIR package manager was created as a decentralized response to the WordPress repository conflict, utilizing federated architecture and cryptographic security. Despite technical success, the project struggled to gain adoption after the WordPress dispute subsided and is now pivoting to support the TYPO3 CMS ecosystem.

item.title: human.json
item.description: The author introduces human.json, a protocol that enables individuals to verify authorship and vouch for the humanity of others using URL-based identity. This decentralized system allows trust to propagate across the web through a crawlable network of cross-site attestations.

item.title: How Can Governments Pay Open Source Maintainers?
item.description: The article explores the complexities government bodies face when attempting to financially support open source software maintainers. It highlights the challenges of integrating public funding into the decentralized and community-driven open source ecosystem.

item.title: Pluralistic: Corrupt anticorruption (14 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: This post discusses the passage of a bipartisan Senate bill aimed at curbing private equity's control over the single-family housing market. Cory Doctorow critiques how historical policy decisions fueled corporate landlordism and exacerbated the housing affordability crisis.

item.title: You Digg?
item.description: The author reflects on the history and eventual second shutdown of the social platform Digg, noting how a rise in AI-powered bot activity rendered the community unsustainable. This experience has led the author to retreat from future social platforms in favor of personal blogging.

item.title: Quoting Jannis Leidel
item.description: Jazzband, an open-source collective, is sunsetting its operations due to the overwhelming volume of AI-generated spam on GitHub. The surge in low-quality automated pull requests has rendered the organization's open-membership model unsustainable in the current software maintenance environment.

item.title: My fireside chat about agentic engineering at the Pragmatic Summit
item.description: Simon Willison shares highlights from his fireside chat at the Pragmatic Summit, focusing on the evolution of AI-assisted software development. He discusses the shift from using simple LLMs to autonomous coding agents that can generate significant portions of a codebase.

item.title: Big tech engineers need big egos
item.description: The article explores the controversial necessity of high self-esteem for engineers in large technology companies. It argues that a strong ego acts as a vital buffer against the inevitable scrutiny and complexity of high-stakes technical environments.

item.title: What do coders do after AI?
item.description: Anil Dash discusses how AI is transforming the role of software developers, distinguishing between corporate layoffs and the actual utility of LLMs. He argues that unlike creative fields where AI consumes the 'soulful' work, coding AI removes drudgery, allowing developers to focus on the human and creative aspects of software engineering.

item.title: Btw: Software, turnkey, beheerd, as a service
item.description: The Dutch Tax and Customs Administration plans to outsource VAT processing to an American firm, raising significant concerns about data sovereignty. Bert Hubert highlights that the core issue is not just using American software, but the full management of these systems by a foreign entity.

item.title: This Week on The Analog Antiquarian
item.description: This entry explores the historical significance and complexities surrounding the trial of Galileo Galilei. It examines the intellectual and religious tensions of the era that led to one of history's most famous conflicts between science and doctrine.

item.title: Dylan Patel — Deep dive on the 3 big bottlenecks to scaling AI compute
item.description: This discussion explores the primary hardware and infrastructure constraints currently limiting AI scalability. It analyzes why high-performance chips like the H100 have significantly appreciated in value due to supply-demand imbalances and technical bottlenecks.

item.title: Forge
item.description: The author introduces 'Forge,' a unified CLI tool designed to standardize interactions across various Git platforms like GitHub, GitLab, and Gitea. By abstracting the inconsistent APIs and command structures of existing tools, Forge provides a consistent interface for human developers and AI agents.

item.title: Everything's Casino
item.description: The article depicts a hypothetical military strike titled 'Operation Epic Fury,' framing modern warfare through the lens of high-stakes gambling. It suggests that international conflicts are increasingly treated with the volatility and risk-taking characteristic of casino games.

item.title: Typesetting sheet music with AI
item.description: The author shares his experience using AI to generate code for Lilypond, a specialized typesetting language for sheet music. Despite the obscurity of the language and limited training data, the AI proves surprisingly effective at creating musical notation for his theory-related posts.

item.title: Windows stack limit checking retrospective: MIPS
item.description: This article explores the technical complexities involved in optimizing stack limit checks within the MIPS architecture. It examines the trade-offs of removing redundant probes to improve system performance.

item.title: Windows stack limit checking retrospective: x86-32, also known as i386
item.description: This article explores the historical implementation of stack limit checking on the x86-32 architecture within Windows. It highlights the unique and unconventional calling conventions used in this legacy environment.

item.title: An odd font rendering bug in Firefox and Safari
item.description: The author identifies a specific font rendering bug affecting the display of certain characters in Firefox and Safari. The issue appears to be browser-specific, as the same content renders correctly in Chrome.

item.title: The web in 1000 lines of C
item.description: The author documents the process of building a minimal web browser in just 1,000 lines of C code. The article demonstrates how to handle core browser functions like HTTP requests, DNS lookups, and HTML parsing to navigate the modern web.

item.title: Pluralistic: Three more AI psychoses (12 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: The article discusses the term 'AI psychosis,' describing how chatbots can induce or amplify delusional behavior in users. It suggests that while this phenomenon is alarming, it should be understood as a continuation of existing psychological patterns, comparing it to historical psychosomatic conditions like Morgellons.

item.title: It's Work that taught me how to think
item.description: The author reflects on their experience dropping out of a computer science program due to the disconnect between academic instruction and real-world application. They highlight how the pressure for grades stifled genuine curiosity, contrasting this with the practical problem-solving skills learned through hands-on work.

item.title: 1M context is now generally available for Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6
item.description: Anthropic has made the 1-million-token context window generally available for Claude 3.5 Opus and Sonnet models. Notably, the company is not charging a premium for long-context usage, differentiating its pricing strategy from competitors like OpenAI and Google.

item.title: Quoting Craig Mod
item.description: Craig Mod shares his experience building a custom accounting tool using AI assistance to solve limitations in off-the-shelf software. The project demonstrates the power of 'vibe-coding,' where LLMs enable rapid, personalized software development that perfectly fits specific professional needs.

item.title: Shopify/liquid: Performance: 53% faster parse+render, 61% fewer allocations
item.description: Shopify CEO Tobias Lütke utilized a coding agent-based approach to perform over 120 automated experiments on the Liquid template engine. These optimizations resulted in a 53% improvement in parse and render speeds and a 61% reduction in memory allocations.

item.title: Restoring an Xserve G5: When Apple built real servers
item.description: Jeff Geerling explores the history and hardware challenges of the Apple Xserve G5, a PowerPC-era server. He highlights the common failure point caused by the 'Capacitor Plague' in the Delta power supply units due to thermal stress.

item.title: Introduction to SQLAlchemy 2 In Practice
item.description: Author Miguel Grinberg is serializing his book 'SQLAlchemy 2 In Practice' for free on his blog. The series serves as a comprehensive guide to using SQLAlchemy 2, the leading Python ORM library, with installments published weekly.

item.title: On Making
item.description: The author reflects on the creative process of making things by hand in the age of AI. They argue that the intrinsic value of creation is often lost when we rely on automation for output.

item.title: Changing my mind on UBI
item.description: The author examines the economic implications of Universal Basic Income (UBI), arguing that market adjustments would likely lead to inflation. They propose a thought experiment where the resulting inefficiency of fiat currency in a UBI-inflated economy leads individuals to revert to commodity-based trading and alternative gold-backed economies.

item.title: Are LLMs not getting better?
item.description: This article examines the ongoing debate regarding the performance progression of Large Language Models. It questions whether the perceived advancements in LLMs are stalling or if current benchmarks are failing to capture true improvements.

item.title: Git Checkout, Reset and Restore
item.description: This post provides a practical comparison between older Git commands like checkout and reset and the newer restore command introduced in Git 2.23. It uses a series of experimental examples to demonstrate how to effectively reset the working tree and index in various scenarios.

item.title: The Elusive Cost Savings of the Prefabricated Home
item.description: This article examines the long-standing belief that modular or prefabricated construction can solve the escalating costs and productivity issues of the home-building industry. It questions whether factory-built homes actually deliver the promised economic efficiencies compared to traditional construction methods.

item.title: Reviewing ENISA’s Package Manager Advisory
item.description: The author reviews ENISA's 2026 technical advisory on package manager security, highlighting its strong risk taxonomy and focus on reachable code. While praising the document's structure, the author critiques some recommendations, such as relying on vanity metrics like GitHub stars, and plans to bridge gaps in cross-platform command guidance.

item.title: Members Only: We desperately need a Reality Literacy
item.description: This article explores the concept of 'Reality Literacy' in an era of digital misinformation and hyper-reality. It argues that developing a refined ability to discern objective truth is essential for navigating modern social and information landscapes.

item.title: Windows 11 after two decades of macOS: okay, but also awful
item.description: The author shares their experience of switching from a high-end Mac Studio to a powerful Windows PC for professional and daily tasks. While they find the Windows setup surprisingly capable and productive, they remain critical of both operating systems, highlighting specific hardware and software frustrations with both platforms.

item.title: How I use generative AI on this blog
item.description: The author discusses his approach to using generative AI in his personal blogging despite having fundamental reservations about the technology. He outlines a strict workflow where AI serves only as an augmented thesaurus or brainstorming tool, ensuring that the final output remains authentic to his own voice.

item.title: Inverse cosine
item.description: This post investigates why the expression sin(arccos(x)) simplifies to √(1 − x²) while other hyperbolic identities do not. The author provides a mathematical proof to demonstrate the validity of this identity for the inverse cosine function.

item.title: The modern formatting addiction in writing
item.description: The author critiques the modern obsession with excessive formatting, such as nested lists and convoluted hierarchies, which complicates reading. By contrasting disjointed fragments with linear, paragraph-based writing, the post argues for prioritizing clarity over structural complexity.

item.title: Historic Energy Price Cap Data (FOI success!)
item.description: The author successfully obtained historical UK energy price cap data from Ofgem by submitting a Freedom of Information request. This post highlights the difficulty of accessing longitudinal public data and provides the recovered dataset for public use.

item.title: MALUS - Clean Room as a Service
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a satirical project called MALUS, which mocks the trend of using AI to 'clean room' open source code to bypass license obligations. The post points out the blurring lines between serious corporate AI strategies and extreme satire.

item.title: Coding After Coders: The End of Computer Programming as We Know It
item.description: This blog post discusses a New York Times article exploring the transformative impact of AI on software development. It highlights the unique ability of programmers to verify AI-generated code through automated testing, distinguishing their field from other professions, while also touching on debates regarding the loss of craftsmanship.

item.title: Quoting Les Orchard
item.description: AI-assisted coding is exposing a long-standing divide among developers between those who value the craft of hand-writing code and those focused primarily on product output. The shift to AI tools makes these diverging motivations visible by forcing developers to choose between manual craftsmanship and machine-directed generation.

item.title: Sorting algorithms
item.description: Simon Willison demonstrates how to use Claude Artifacts to generate interactive, animated visualizations of various sorting algorithms. He showcases the process of using AI to code these animations, including Python's complex Timsort algorithm, by refining prompts and referencing source code.

item.title: Can the MacBook Neo replace my M4 Air?
item.description: The author evaluates whether the MacBook Neo can serve as a suitable travel companion, drawing on his history of preferring portable, lightweight laptops. He contrasts the 'Neo' against his high-performance M4 Max Mac Studio desktop setup.

item.title: Every minute you aren’t running 69 agents, you are falling behind
item.description: The author critiques the current social media-driven fear regarding AI adoption and emphasizes that AI is an evolutionary tool rather than magic. He encourages readers to focus on creating genuine value instead of engaging in zero-sum games or anxiety-inducing trends.

item.title: I’m glad the Anthropic fight is happening now
item.description: This piece discusses the current conflicts and high-stakes negotiations surrounding AI development at companies like Anthropic. It frames these internal and industry-wide disputes as critical precursors to the global governance of advanced AI.

item.title: git-pkgs/actions
item.description: git-pkgs has introduced reusable GitHub Actions to streamline dependency management, vulnerability scanning, license auditing, and SBOM generation within CI/CD pipelines. These actions simplify the process of monitoring project dependencies directly through pull request automation and integration with GitHub's security tools.

item.title: How do compilers ensure that large stack allocations do not skip over the guard page?
item.description: This post explains the stack probe mechanism used by compilers to prevent large stack allocations from jumping over the guard page. It highlights that the compiler must perform incremental allocations to ensure the guard page is triggered correctly.

item.title: I don't know if I like working at higher levels of abstraction
item.description: The author reflects on the growing influence of AI tools in software development, which consistently drive developers toward higher levels of abstraction. The post questions the hidden costs and potential loss of fundamental understanding associated with this shift.

item.title: Game Review: It Takes Two ★★★★★
item.description: The author reviews the cooperative game 'It Takes Two,' using a humorous take on its plot about a divorcing couple. The post serves as part of the author's personal goal to engage in more co-operative gaming with their spouse.

item.title: Pluralistic: AI "journalists" prove that media bosses don't give a shit (11 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow highlights Ed Zitron's critique of the tech industry, focusing on how media and hardware companies exploit non-technical users. The post argues that the proliferation of AI-generated content and bloated, underpowered hardware reflects a systemic indifference toward consumer experience.

item.title: Where did you think the training data was coming from?
item.description: This article discusses the erosion of personal privacy in modern computing, highlighting how major tech companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Google leverage telemetry and licensing agreements to collect user data for AI training. It argues that users can no longer assume their devices are private, drawing parallels between current practices and past security concerns.

item.title: The Server Older than my Kids!
item.description: The author reflects on the evolution of their blog's server infrastructure, recounting how a viral post inspired them to optimize file delivery. After years of procrastination, they are finally modernizing their setup by creating provisioning scripts to replace manual, undocumented configuration processes.

item.title: From the DF Archive: ‘And Oranges’
item.description: John Gruber revisits his 20-year-old essay regarding the high standards and critical mindset of Mac users. The post reflects on the enduring belief that Mac users choose Apple products not for perfection, but because they are superior to the alternatives.

item.title: ★ The MacBook Neo
item.description: The article reflects on how Apple's silicon transition from Intel to ARM was predictable based on the performance trends of A-series chips dating back to the iPhone 6S. It highlights the emergence of the 'MacBook Neo,' a budget-friendly laptop powered by the iPhone 16 Pro's A18 Pro chip, demonstrating that Apple's mobile processors are now mature enough to anchor the consumer Mac lineup.

item.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
item.description: An Iran-linked hacktivist group has claimed responsibility for a disruptive wiper attack on the global medical technology firm Stryker. The incident has caused significant operational disruptions, including forcing thousands of employees in Ireland to stay home and triggering a declared building emergency at the company's U.S. headquarters.

item.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
item.description: Microsoft's March 2026 Patch Tuesday addresses 77 security vulnerabilities across its software ecosystem. While no zero-day flaws were identified this month, the release includes critical updates that require prompt attention for organizational security.

item.title: Quoting John Carmack
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a quote from John Carmack regarding software architecture. The quote emphasizes that designing for future requirements rarely yields positive results, reinforcing the YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It) principle.

item.title: AI should help us produce better code
item.description: Simon Willison argues that the decline in software quality often attributed to AI coding tools is a human choice rather than an inevitable outcome. He advocates for using AI to proactively address technical debt and time-consuming refactoring tasks to ensure better overall code quality.

item.title: No, it doesn't cost Anthropic $5k per Claude Code user
item.description: This article debunks the viral claim that Anthropic loses $5,000 per Claude Code subscriber. The author performs a factual analysis to demonstrate why such high-cost estimates are inaccurate.
